content.full_text:
March 5, 1976

Bartley C. Deamer  
McCutchen, Doyle, Brown & Enerson  
601 California Street  
San Francisco CA 94105

Dear Mr. Deamer:

Thank you for your letter of February 20, 1976, requesting a clarification with respect to one portion of the chairman's response to your earlier letter of November 12, 1975.

Your earlier letter did not indicate that the $1,000 earned on the California Matter was the result of an allocation on the employee's regular salary based on activities performed primarily in New York, and this assumption was not made in responding to your question. To the contrary, it was assumed that the $1,000 was earned separately for work on the California matter.

Your understanding of the third sentence of Section 18239(e)(2)(B) is correct, and it is not necessary to apportion wages received as a full-time employee engaged in activities other than influencing or attempting to influence California legislative or administrative action to determine the applicability of the $1,000 test. Accordingly, if the $1,000 earned by the employee engaged in activities primarily in New York was part of his regular salary and he merely became involved in a California matter for which he was not separately compensated, he would not become a lobbyist by reason of his involvement in the California matter.

I assume the foregoing is responsive to your need for clarification. If, however, you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Kenneth Finney  
Chief  
Legal Division

KF:pvp
